coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly PS@dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe coupled with core-shell structured MIP for the detection of malachite green in fish
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ran, Hui
Lin, Zheng-zhong
Hong, Cheng-yi
Zeng, Jun
Yao, Qiu-Hong
Huang, Zhi-Yong
coredata.dc:description: Rapid detection of malachite green (MG) by fluorescence probes of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) coated with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) has been reported, but low sensitivity, slow response and narrow linear range were the main disadvantages for these MIP@QDs probes. Here, an ultra-sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe based on self-assembly functionalized polystyrene (PS) and core-shell structured MIP was developed for the detection of MG in fish. After modified with poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, CdTe QDs and silica encapsulated carbon quantum dots (CDs@SiO2) with negative charges, used as detection and reference fluorophores, were respectively self-assembled on the surface of PS to form PS-QDs&CDs. And a core-shell structured MIP layer was fabricated on the surface of PS-QDs&CDs to form a ratiometric fluorescence probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs with sol-gel method using MG, (3-aminopropyl)triethoxy-silan and tetraethyl orthosilicate as template, functional monomer and cross-linker, respectively. With intensive fluorescence densities of QDs&CDs in nucleus, the fluorescence emission of probe at λem of 630 nm could be linearly quenched by MG, while the fluorescence emission at λem of 430 nm remained unchanged. The probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs owned good fluorescence stability (RSD ≤2.3%), fast response time (5 min), wide linear range (0.1–32 μmol L−1) and low detection limit (5.6 μg kg−1), and has been applied to the detection of MG in fish samples with recoveries of 94.3–110.2%. The result validated that the as-prepared probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs had an excellent performance for MG detection in fish samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-assembly
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Malachite green
Fish

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs, CDs, CDs@SiO2 and PS
Detection of MG in fish samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic procedures of the preparation and recognition of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs.
TEM images of PS (aand PS-QDs (b,c). Insets showed the distribution of particle sizes.
TEM images (a), size distributions (b) and EDS spectrum (c) of PS-QDs-CDs.
TEM images of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs (a) and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs (c). EDS spectra of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs (b) and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs (d). Insets show the distribution of particle sizes.
FT-IR spectra of CDs@SiO2, PS, PS-QDs, MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs.

(a)The fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs, PS-QDs, CDs, CDs@SiO2 and PS-QDs&CDs. (b)The UV–vis absorption spectra of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs, MG and the mixture of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and MG (The concentrations of MG in acetonitrile were 2, 5, 10, 20 and 30 μmol L−1, respectively). (c) The fluorescence spectrum of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG in acetonitrile. (d) The fluorescence decay profiles of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs in the absence and presence of 10 μmol L−1 of MG.
Adsorption kinetics of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG (a), and the isothermal adsorption curves of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG (b).
Fluorescence spectra of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs (a) and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs (b) mixed with MG at different concentrations.
The relationships between I 630/430 and the concentrations of MG.

Selectivity of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs (A) and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs (C) to MG, LMG, CV, LCV, MV, PRL and RL, respectively. Interference tests of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs (B) and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs (D) in binary solutions of MG mixed with CV, LCV, LMG, MV, PRL and RL, respectively. The different letters (a,b,c,d) represent the significant differences at p < 0.05. The concentrations of each quencher were 10 μmol L−1.
The zeta potential values of CDs, CDs@SiO2, CdTe QDs, PS, PS-PDDA, PS-QDs, PS-QDs-CTAB and PS-QDs-CDs.
Recovery tests of MG in fish samples measured with the probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs.(n = 3).
Comparison of various MIP-QDs methods for MG detection.

row.entry: Synthesis of dual templates of CdTe@MIP

head.ce:title: Self-assembly PS@dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe coupled with core-shell structured MIP for the detection of malachite green in fish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen, 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Collaborative Innovation Center for Exploitation and Utilization of Marine Biological Resources, Xiamen, 361102, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Collaborative Innovation Center for Exploitation and Utilization of Marine Biological Resources

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen, 361021, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid detection of malachite green (MG) by fluorescence probes of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) coated with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) has been reported, but low sensitivity, slow response and narrow linear range were the main disadvantages for these MIP@QDs probes. Here, an ultra-sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe based on self-assembly functionalized polystyrene (PS) and core-shell structured MIP was developed for the detection of MG in fish. After modified with poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, CdTe QDs and silica encapsulated carbon quantum dots (CDs@SiO2) with negative charges, used as detection and reference fluorophores, were respectively self-assembled on the surface of PS to form PS-QDs&CDs. And a core-shell structured MIP layer was fabricated on the surface of PS-QDs&CDs to form a ratiometric fluorescence probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs with sol-gel method using MG, (3-aminopropyl)triethoxy-silan and tetraethyl orthosilicate as template, functional monomer and cross-linker, respectively. With intensive fluorescence densities of QDs&CDs in nucleus, the fluorescence emission of probe at λem of 630 nm could be linearly quenched by MG, while the fluorescence emission at λem of 430 nm remained unchanged. The probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs owned good fluorescence stability (RSD ≤2.3%), fast response time (5 min), wide linear range (0.1–32 μmol L−1) and low detection limit (5.6 μg kg−1), and has been applied to the detection of MG in fish samples with recoveries of 94.3–110.2%. The result validated that the as-prepared probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs had an excellent performance for MG detection in fish samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG) was once used as an antimicrobial and antiparasitic drug in aquaculture [1], but had been banned in aquaculture usage by many countries due to the potential harm to human health [2]. However, MG is occasionally found to be illegally used because of its high efficiency and low-cost [3]. Although many analytical methods have been applied for MG detection [4–9], the development of rapid, reliable and inexpensive detection method is still a great challenge.

With specific recognition and excellent optical properties, fluorescence probes based on quantum dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been developed for the detection of small target molecules including MG. For example, the probes of MIP-coated CdTe quantum dots prepared by various approaches have been reported for the detection of MG in fish samples based on the strategy of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [10–12], in which several CdTe QDs with sizes of 3–5 nm embedded with MIP layers were used as luminescent sources. The particles of MIP-coated CdTe were usually prepared with broad size distributions ranging from 40 nm to 300 nm, in which QDs were deeply embedded in thick MIP layers in the probe particles [13–15]. Some disadvantages such as insufficient sensitivity, poor selectivity and slow mass transfer occurred due to the densely packed and trapped QDs and the thick MIP layers. A small amount of QDs as the luminescent sources should be chosen in each of the particles because the high densities of QDs trapped in MIP-coated CdTe might result in fluorescence quenching due to inner effect, which would reduce the source fluorescence intensities. And a thick MIP layer would further decrease the signal intensities and increase the mass transfer resistances of target molecules to MIP binding sites, which would inevitably result in low response speed, poor sensitivity and weak selectivity for detection.

It has been demonstrated that the thickness of MIP layer can be well controlled by the formation of a core-shell structure MIPs, and a thin MIP layer may play crucial roles in the design and construction of analytical sensors or devices by integrating MIP layer on the surface of functional cores [16,17]. For example, the core-shell magnetic surface imprinted polymers (Fe3O4@SiO2@MIPs) have been successfully prepared for the extraction of tetrabromobisphenol A from water with good adsorption capacity, high recognition ability and fast kinetics [18]. An uniform core-shell molecularly imprinted polymer (Fe3O4@MIP) was prepared and has been successfully utilized for the enrichment of 2,4-D from tap water and Chinese cabbage samples [19].

Micron-sized mono-dispersed polystyrene (PS) microspheres, with relatively large surface areas, easy surface modification and controllable particle sizes, have been used in many fields such as immuno-medicine, biochemistry and analytical chemistry [20]. In the present study, PS microspheres with negative charge were synthesized. After modified with strong cationic polymer, the surface of PS was positively charged, and the CdTe QDs with negative charge could self-assemble on the surface by electrostatic interaction to form PS-QDs. After re-modified with a cationic surface active agent, the surface of PS-QDs was parceled by micellae with positive charge, and silica encapsulated carbon quantum dots (Labeled as CDs@SiO2) with negative charge could self-assemble on PS-QDs to form a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe (PS-QDs&CDs) which would result in enhanced fluorescence densities of detection signal and reference signal. Sequentially, a thin MIP layer was coated onto the surface of PS-QDs&CDs to construct a core-shell MIP@PS-QDs&CDs. As a novel optical probe, the MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was used to detect trace MG in fish samples by ratiometric fluorescence method with excellent detection performance.

Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), malachite green (MG), (NH4)2S2O8 and NaBH4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tellurium powder, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), 1-hexanol, TritonX-100, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxy-silan (APTES) and poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA, 35%) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CdCl2·2.5H2O, NaOH, crystalviolet (CV), leucocrystal violet (LCV), leucomalachite green (LMG), styrene (St), cyclohexane, acetone, ethyl alcohol and acetonitrile were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Methyl violet (MV), pararosaniline (PRL) and rosaniline (RL) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were in analytical grade, and were used without any further purification. Water used in all the experiments was purified with TKA Ultra (18.2 MΩ cm, Germany).

Fluorescence measurement was performed with a LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA). The absorption spectra were measured with a Lambda 265 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA). The morphology and size of fluorescence probe were characterized by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, Japan). The element composition of fluorescence probe was analyzed with an X-MaxN 80 T energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) (Oxford, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientiﬁc Fisher, US). Fluorescence lifetime was measured with a FLS 980-STM steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh instruments Ltd., UK). Zeta potentials were analyzed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs, CDs, CDs@SiO2 and PS
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs and CDs were prepared according to our previous reports [21,22]. For CdTe QDs preparation, 3 mL ultra-pure grade water, 1 mmol tellurium powder and 2 mmol NaBH4 were mixed and stirred at 800 rpm for 55 min under nitrogen atmosphere as precursor solution. Simultaneously, 2 mmol of CdCl2·2.5H2O, 100 mL of ultrapure water and 4.8 mmol MPA were respectively added into a 250 mL flask to obtain a homogeneous solution. After adjusting pH value to 6.0 with NaOH, the solution was stirred at 800 rpm for 30 min at room temperature under nitrogen atmosphere. Then, the precursor solution was added and sequentially stirred for another 30 min under nitrogen atmosphere. The mixture was sealed into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and kept at 160 °C for 50 min. After cooling to room temperature, the MPA stabilized CdTe QDs were precipitated with acetone and washed twice with ethanol, then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 4 h.

For CDs preparation, 0.5 g of anhydrous citric acid and urea were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and were transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon-lined vessel, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 4 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black-green solution was precipitated with acetone thrice and dried under vacuum.

CDs@SiO2 was prepared by a reverse micro-emulsion method with a minor modification [23]. Firstly, 1.8 mL of n-hexanol, 7.5 mL of cyclohexane and 1.77 mL of Triton X-100 were mixed in a 100 mL-flask and stirred for 20 min. Then, 2 mg CDs dispersed in 1 mL water was added into the mixture. After stirring for 10 min, 60 μL of ammonia and 100 μL of TEOS were respectively dropped into the mixture, and the reaction continued for another two hours under stirring. After adding 100 μL of APTES, the reaction of micro-emulsion system was sustained for 24 h. Then, 10 mL acetone was added for demulsification, and the precipitate was obtained by centrifugation and was washed with ethanol and acetonitrile (7:3, v/v) thrice. After oven-dried at 60 °C for 4 h, the CDs@SiO2 were obtained.

PS microspheres were prepared according to the previous report with some modification [24]. In short, 2 mL styrene monomer was injected into 80 mL water, and dispersed by sonic oscillation. After deoxygenization with N2 for 15 min, 10 mg of (NH4)2S2O8 was added as an initiator, and the reaction was carried out in water bath at 70 °C for 5 h. White precipitate of PS was separated and washed thrice with water. After dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h, the PS microspheres were obtained.

0.15 g PS was ultrasonically dispersed in 30 mL water, and 1 mL PDDA (35%) solution was added. After shaking vigorously for 30 min and washing thrice with water, 10 mL of CdTe QDs (0.12 g mL−1) was added, and the solution was stirred for 1 h. The PS-QDs was obtained by centrifugation, and was washed thrice with water.

The MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was synthesized with a sol-gel method [25,26] with some modification. Briefly, 24 mL of anhydrous alcohol, 4 mL of PS-QDs dispersed in anhydrous alcohol, 1 mL of CTAB (0.06 mol L−1 in water) and 200 μL NaOH (2 mol L−1) were mixed in a round-bottomed flask. After stirring for 20 min, 1 mL of CDs@SiO2 (1 mg in anhydrous alcohol) was added into the reaction system. Then, TEOS (150 μL), APTES (30 μL) and MG (9 mg) were respectively added. After adding 60 μL of ammonium hydroxide, the mixture was stirred for 24 h in darkness for polymerization. The obtained MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was washed with ethanol and acetonitrile until no MG and CTAB could be detected at 618 nm and 230 nm respectively, and was oven-dried in vacuum before use. NIP@PS-QDs&CDs was synthesized with the same steps but in the absence of MG.

The fluorescence intensities of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs were measured at 430 nm and 630 nm simultaneously under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The values of I 630/430 were calculated with Eq. (1):
(1)
I
630 / 430
=
F
630 / 430
M G = 0
F
630 / 430
M G
Where
F
630 / 430
M G = 0
was the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 630 nm and 430 nm respectively in the absence of MG.
F
630 / 430
M G
was the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 630 nm and 430 nm respectively in the presence of MG at different concentrations.

The fluorescence lifetimes of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs were observed in the absence and presence of 10 μmol L−1 MG, respectively. And the data were calculated with Eq. (2) [27]:
(2)
F ( t ) =
B 1
⋅ exp ( − t /
τ 1
) +
B 2
⋅ exp ( − t /
τ 2
)
Here, F(t) was the fluorescence intensity of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs corresponding to the time at t; τ1and τ2 were the radiative and nonradiative lifetimes of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs, respectively. B1 and B2 were the corresponding statistical weights [28]. The average fluorescence lifetime (τav) was calculated according to Eq. (3) [29]:
(3)
τ av
=
∑
B i
⋅ τ
i 2
∑
B i
⋅ τ i
where τi was the measured lifetime, Bi represented the value of statistical weight.
After dispersed in ethanol, PS, PS-QDs, PS-QDs-CDs, MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs were respectively immersed in copper meshes for 10 s and then air-dried (1 h) for TEM image recorded.
PS, PS-QDs, CDs@SiO2, MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs were respectively mixed and ground with KBr (1:100, w/w) for tableting, and were recorded for FT-IR spectra.
Zeta potentials of CDs, CDs@SiO2, CdTe QDs, PS, PDDA modified PS, PS-QDs, CTAB modified PS-QDs and PS-QDs-CDs in aqueous solutions were measured at room temperature.

Each of 50 mg of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs were dispersed in 8 mL acetonitrile containing MG at room temperature for the testes of adsorption kinetics and isothermal adsorption. After adsorption and centrifugation, the adsorption capacities of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs were calculated according to Eq. (4) [30]:
(4)
Q =
M ⋅ V ⋅
C 1
−
C 2
m
Where Q (mg g−1) was the adsorption capacities of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs or NIP@PS-QDs&CDs. M (g mol-1) was the absolute molecular mass of MG. V (L) was the volume of adsorption system. C1 and C2 (μmol L−1) were the initial and final concentrations of MG in supernatants, respectively. m (g) was the mass of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs or NIP@PS-QDs&CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MG in fish samples
ce:section.ce:para: Farmed sturgeon, bass and grass carp were purchased from a local supermarket. The sample pretreatment was performed according to a previous report with some modification [31]. In brief, tissue-triturated fish samples were mixed with MG in acetonitrile at concentrations of 2, 5, and 10 μmol L−1 for recovery tests. After centrifugation, the supernatant was transferred to a centrifuge tube. The extractions were repeated three times, and the supernatants were combined. Each of the sample solution was mixed with 50 μL (0.2 mg) of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs for measurement.

The schematic diagram of the preparation of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was shown in Fig. 1 . The zeta potential of PS microspheres was −39 mV (Table 1 ) in the present study. After modified with strong cation polyelectrolyte of PDDA, the surface of PS was positively charged with a potential value of + 70 mV. Therefore, CdTe QDs with negative charge of -34.3 mV was spontaneously adsorbed onto the surface of the modified PS by electrostatic attraction, yielding the particles of PS-QDs. After removing the excess of unbound QDs, CdTe QDs were solidly loaded on the surface of PS with a zeta potential of -6.88 mV, enabling strong emission of fluorescence signal for MG detection. The preparation conditions of PS-QDs were optimized. After centrifugation, the color of PS-QDs in residue should be bright red while the supernatant was colorless because the white PS particles have been completely wrapped by CdTe QDs and no extra QDs left in solutions as shown in Fig. S1. The optimal preparation conditions of PS-QDs were 0.15 g of PS, 1% (wt%) of PDDA and 0.12 g of CdTe QDs. The TEM images in Fig. 2 showed that the morphology of PS was uniform and smooth (Fig. 2a), but the surface became rough and granular after loaded with QDs (Fig. 2b and c). However, the diameters of PS before and after loading QDs were similar with the values of 350 nm and 355 nm for PS and PS-QDs, respectively. The result of TEM images in Fig. 2 illustrated that CdTe QDs were successfully self-assembled on the surface of PDDA-modified PS.

Although CDs prepared in the present study were negatively charged with a zeta potential of -25.6 mV as shown in Table 1, the CDs could not be directly loaded onto the surface of the PDDA modified PS because the fluorescence of CDs might be quenched by PDDA based on our measurement. Therefore, CDs were previously encapsulated with SiO2 with a Stöber method [32]. After encapsulation, the CDs@SiO2 were negatively charged (-28.8 mV) and emitted strong fluorescence at 430 nm. And CDs@SiO2 was conceived to simultaneously load onto the surface of PS-PDDA like that of CdTe QDs. However, the loaded amount of CDs@SiO2 on PS-PDDA was much less compared with those of CdTe QDs, leading to a low fluorescence emission at 430 nm. Therefore, a micellar surface of PS-QDs was modified with CTAB (a cationic surfactant) before loading of CDs@SiO2, and the micellar surface was positively charged with 21.2 mV of zeta potential. After loading with CDs@SiO2, a dual-emission fluorophores on PS surface, labeled as PS-QDs&CDs, were constructed. TEM image in Fig. 3 a and b showed that the diameter of PS-QDs&CDs was 362 nm which was greater compared with that of PS-QDs (Fig. 2b and c). And slight adhesion among the particles of PS-QDs&CDs was observed compared with that of PS-QDs. In addition, the EDS spectrum (Fig. 3c) showed that the particles of PS-QDs&CDs contained elements of Si, C, S, Cd and Te, further verifying the successful self-assembly of CDs@SiO2 on PS-QDs via CTAB.

The probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was prepared using PS-QDs&CDs as luminous core, in which MG, APTES and TEOS were used as template, functional monomer and cross-linker, respectively. The synthetic conditions in Fig. S2 showed that the optimum molar ratio of MG, APTES and TEOS was 1:3:15, and the mass ratio of PS-QDs and CDs@SiO2 was 9:1.

TEM images of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs in Fig. 4 a and c showed that the two particles were spherical with average diameters of 383 nm and 384 nm, respectively. The elements of Si, O, C, etc. in EDS spectra (Fig. 4b and d) confirmed the formation of core-shell structured MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs.

The FT-IR spectra of PS, CDs@SiO2, PS-QDs, MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs were shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks at 3176 cm−1 (NH stretching vibration), 1636 cm−1, 1560 cm−1 and 697 cm-1 (NH bending vibration) confirmed the presence of APTES [33]. The characteristic peaks at 1053 cm-1 (Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching vibration), 756 cm−1 (Si-O-Si symmetrical stretching vibration) and 456 cm−1 (Si-O-Si bending vibration) verified the existence of TEOS [34]. Two obvious vibration peaks at 2924 cm-1 (C–H symmetrical stretching vibration) and 2849 cm-1 (C–H asymmetric stretching vibration) could be presumed to be from the methylene and methyl groups in the macromolecular chain. The characteristic vibration peaks at 1601 cm−1, 1492 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 might derive from the deformation vibration of benzene ring skeleton [35]. The peaks at 756 cm-1 and 697 cm-1 might also attribute to the C–H bending vibration of benzene ring. In addition, the peaks at 1745 cm−1 (CO stretching vibration) and 1354 cm−1 (C–H bending vibration in-plane) demonstrated the presence of mercaptopropionic acid stabilized CdTe QDs [36]. Some fine spectral peaks in PS disappeared after binding to QDs, indicating the formation of PS-QDs. Similar characteristic peaks of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs were observed after removal of MG, which further indicated that the template MG was washed completely.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching between MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and MG was illustrated in Fig. 6 . After loading with CdTe QDs, the fluorescence of PS-QDs was similar to that of CdTe QDs. And the fluorescence of CDs@SiO2 was also similar to that of CDs as shown in Fig. 6a. In addition, after self-assembly of CDs@SiO2 on PS-QDs via CTAB, the PS-QDs&CDs presented two fluorescence emissions at 630 nm and 430 nm respectively, indicating that the fluorophores of CdTe QDs and CDs@SiO2 in the core of probe did not interact and the fluorescence emissions were strong at the two positions. The respective titration tests of CDs and CDs@SiO2 with CdTe QDs and PS-QDs as shown in Fig.S3 further demonstrated that the two fluorophores were stable in the core of probe. UV–vis absorption spectra of MG, MIP@PS-QDs&CDs, and the mixture of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and MG were shown in Fig. 6b. There was no obvious absorption peak for the probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs, but three spectral peaks were observed in the mixture of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and MG, which were similar to those of MG solutions. The phenomenon confirmed that the binding of MG on the surface of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was mainly due to the physical adsorption. As shown in Fig. 6c, the probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs has two fluorescence emissions locating at 630 nm and 430 nm respectively, in which the fluorescence emission at 630 nm was overlapped with the absorption spectrum of MG. The spectral overlap might be used to explain the fluorescence quenching effect of the MIP@PS-QDs&CDs probe by MG. While the fluorescence emission of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs at 430 nm remained unchanged, and could be used as the reference fluorophore in the ratiometric fluorescence detection. It should be noted that the fluorescence emission of probe at 430 nm was also overlapped with a small absorption peak of MG at the same position, but the fluorescence could not be quenched by MG. The result in Fig. 6d showed that the fluorescence lifetime of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was 12.8 ns, while the value decreased to 10.5 ns after mixed the MIP@PS-QDs&CDs with MG. The obvious change of fluorescence lifetime indicated that the fluorescence quenching of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs by MG might be due to FRET.

The adsorption kinetics and isotherm of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG were tested as shown in Fig. 7 . The adsorption equilibrium of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG reached within 5 min as shown in Fig. 7a. The rapid adsorption of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG might be due to the thin MIP layer in which the specific binding cavities were distributed on the surface. The MIP layer was about 8 nm calculated based on the average diameters of PS-QDs&CDs and MIP@PS-QDs&CDs as shown in Figs. 3b and 4 a, respectively. The thickness of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was much thinner than those of our previous reports in which the diameters of MIP@QDs ranged from 22.5 nm to 145 nm [11,12]. Therefore, the adsorption rate of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG was remarkably faster than those of the previous reports. The adsorption isotherm indicated that the adsorption capacity of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs to MG was 145 mg g−1(Fig.7b). The high adsorption capacity of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs indicated that there existed specific recognition sites on the MIP layer compared with that of NIP@PS-QDs&CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs mixed with MG at different concentrations were shown in Fig. 8 . Except for the constant of fluorescence at 430 nm for MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs, only MIP@PS-QDs&CDs presented a linear quenching effect at 630 nm by MG at different concentrations. The results further confirmed that the tailor-made recognition sites distributed on the surface of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs, and MIPs@PS-QDs&CDs could be used as a probe to detect MG by ratiometric fluorescence method. As shown in Fig. 9 , the values of I 630/430 were linearly related to MG concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 32 μmol L−1. The equation of I 630/430 = 0.50C+0.94 (R 2 = 0.998) was suitable for low MG level (0.1 to 1 μmol L−1), and I 630/430 = 0.061C+1.4 (R2 = 0.994) was fitted for high MG level (1 to 32 μmol L−1).

Several analogues such as leucocrystal violet (LCV), crystal violet (CV), rosaniline (RL), methyl violet (MV), pararosaniline (PRL) and leucomalachite green (LMG) which have similar chemical structures (Fig.S4) to MG were selected to evaluate the selectivity and interferences. The UV–vis absorption spectra of MG, LMG, CV, LCV, RL, MV and PRL were shown in Fig.S5, in which the spectra of CV (580 nm), RL (542 nm), MV (576 nm) and PRL (538 nm) were similar to that of MG (618 nm), and the spectra of LCV (270 nm) and LMG (260 nm) were different from MG. The fluorescence spectra of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs and NIP@PS-QDs&CDs to these single chemicals and the binary solutions (MG mixed with another chemical) were respectively shown in Fig.S7. Results in Fig. 10 A showed that the I 630/430 of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was significantly higher (p < 0.05) to MG detection than those of the analogues although these analogues have similar chemical structure and similar spectra. While almost no selectivity was observed for NIP@PS-QDs&CDs (Fig. 10C). The results indicated that the cavities in MIP layer enabled the probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs to bind MG molecules specifically. Interference tests in binary solutions of MG mixed respectively with CV, LCV, LMG, MV, PRL and RL showed that there was no interference for MG detection by MIP@PS-QDs&CDs except for CV and MV with some interferences as shown in Fig. 10B. It could be concluded that the probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs processed relatively high selectivity for MG detection.

The prepared MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was applied to the detection of MG in fish samples. The results in Table 2 showed that the recoveries ranged from 94.3% to 110.2%, indicating that the prepared probe could accurately detect MG in fish samples. Nine parallel sample blank tests were performed and the standard deviation (σ) was 0.0013. A detection limit of the analytical method calculated based on three times of σ was 5.6 μg kg−1 as described in Table S1. A comparison of MG detection based on molecularly imprinted techniques was listed in Table 3 . Compared with those of the previous reports based on the methods of MIP@QDs, this work presented an excellent detection limit and linear range. In addition, the probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs showed faster response rate than those of previous reports [11,12]. These excellent performance of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs probe might be attributed to the high fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and CDs@SiO2 on the surface of PS through electrostatic interaction, and the core-shell structure with specific sites uniformly distributing in the thin MIP layer. The properties provided the probe with high sensitivity, rapid response, and broad linear range. Besides, the low RSD values (Table 3) indicated that the MIP@PS-QDs&CDs probe presented better detection stability compared with those of the previous reports, which might be attributed to the ratiometric fluorescence detection mode.

An ultra-sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe of MIP@PS-QDs&CDs was developed by a sol-gel method using PS-QDs&CDs as optical sources. The probe presented wide linear range (0.1–32 μmol L−1) of MG detection and low detection limit (5.6 μg kg−1). The excellent recovery and low RSDs (≤2.3%) verified that the as-prepared MIP@PS-QDs&CDs could be used as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the rapid detection of MG in fish samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Foundation from the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China (2016Y0064), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2018J01432, 2017J01633), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Xiamen, China (3502Z20183031), Innovative Research Team of Jimei University, China (2010A007), and the National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (201610390042, 201710390022, 201810390071, 20181xj008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.12.029.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
